_metadata.description: Global Space IXP (1200 km) — a hypothetical constellation of Internet Exchange Point satellites designed to interconnect disparate LEO constellations via inter-satellite links, co-altitude with OneWeb and IRIS².
sources: Derived from altitude analysis of Starlink, Kuiper, OneWeb, Telesat Lightspeed, IRIS², Guowang and Qianfan constellation parameters.

design_rationale.altitude_selection: 1200 km places the IXP co-altitude with OneWeb Gen-1 and IRIS² LEO shells. This enables zero-radial-separation ISL to those constellations while keeping the upper LEO shells (Telesat 1325 km) within 125 km radial distance. Trade-off: 675 km radial separation down to the dense Starlink/Kuiper cluster.
design_rationale.inclination_selection: 86.4° near-polar orbit ensures every IXP orbit crosses all other constellation orbital planes regardless of their inclination (33°–98.98°).
design_rationale.walker_star_pattern: Walker Star 48/6/1 — 6 planes with 30° RAAN spacing and a phase offset factor of 1. At 1200 km the equatorial coverage cells are ~3 964 km (cross-track) × 5 946 km (along-track), yielding worst-case slant distance of ~3 573 km.

notes: This is a reference/hypothetical constellation for simulation purposes.
All IXP satellites are assumed to carry multi-beam optical ISL terminals capable of simultaneous links to satellites in different constellations.
Co-altitude with OneWeb/IRIS² minimises ISL acquisition complexity for those constellations.
Higher altitude increases coverage footprint per satellite but increases latency to the Starlink/Kuiper cluster.

shells.notes: Single-shell near-polar Walker Star constellation at 1200 km. Co-altitude with OneWeb Gen-1 and IRIS² LEO shells.

derived_orbital_parameters.description: Pre-computed orbital parameters for the IXP shell

isl_reach.description: Maximum line-of-sight ISL distance and one-way propagation delay from the IXP shell to each target constellation altitude.

coverage_geometry.description: Coverage cell geometry at the equator for the Walker Star 48/6/1 constellation at 1200 km.
coverage_geometry.notes: At higher latitudes the orbit planes converge, reducing cross-track spacing and improving coverage. Polar regions have excellent coverage due to orbital plane convergence.
